Sentiment Dashboard

Try the live tool All Documentation Back to Portfolio
Project Documentation

Real Time Sentiment Dashboard

A full walkthrough of how this AI powered sentiment analysis tool was designed, built, and deployed — including the API choices made, content moderation approach, and known limitations of keyword based news searching.

1

Overview

The Real Time Sentiment Dashboard allows users to enter any topic or keyword and instantly see the current sentiment in the news — broken down into positive, negative, and neutral categories with interactive charts and an AI generated summary.

This was built as the second portfolio project to demonstrate real world data retrieval, natural language processing, and data visualisation skills. Rather than analysing static data, this tool pulls live news articles at the moment of each search, making the results genuinely current and dynamic.

The tool uses a two layer approach — NewsAPI retrieves recent articles and Claude AI analyses their sentiment and generates a contextual summary. This combination means the results are not just raw scores but include intelligent commentary on what the data actually means.

2

Tech Stack

NewsAPI

Retrieves up to 10 recent English language news articles for any keyword, sorted by publication date. Free tier allows 100 requests per day.

Claude API (Anthropic)

Analyses the sentiment of retrieved articles, assigns positive, negative, or neutral labels, filters irrelevant content, and generates a contextual summary.

Chart.js

Renders two interactive charts — a doughnut chart showing the sentiment breakdown as percentages and a bar chart showing the raw article count per category.

Django

Handles routing, form processing, session based usage tracking for guests, and database usage tracking for registered users.

3

Architecture

The dashboard is built as a separate Django app called sentiment. The logic is split into four clean functions — each doing one specific job. This makes the code easy to debug and easy to swap out individual components, such as replacing NewsAPI with a different news source in the future.

Request Flow 

User enters a keyword
      ↓
Django validates input and checks blocked terms list
      ↓
Guest: check session usage count (max 3 free)
Registered: unlimited access
      ↓
NewsAPI fetches up to 10 recent articles
      ↓
Articles formatted and sent to Claude with structured prompt
      ↓
Claude returns sentiment scores and summary
      ↓
Scores converted to percentages
      ↓
Usage count incremented in session or database
      ↓
Results passed to template
      ↓
Chart.js renders doughnut and bar charts in the browser

Function Structure 

load_blocked_terms()           — reads blocked_terms.txt file
get_news_articles(keyword)     — fetches articles from NewsAPI
analyse_sentiment_with_claude() — sends articles to Claude API
parse_claude_response()        — extracts scores and summary
sentiment(request)             — main view tying everything together
4

Step by Step Build

01 Set Up NewsAPI

Created a free account at newsapi.org and obtained an API key. The key was added to the .env file locally and to Vercel's environment variables for production. The newsapi-python library was installed to provide a clean Python interface to the API.

02 Create the Sentiment Django App

Created a dedicated sentiment app following the same pattern as the summariser — its own views.py, urls.py, and templates folder. This keeps all sentiment logic self contained and separate from the rest of the site.

03 Build the Content Moderation System

Rather than hardcoding a list of blocked terms in the Python code, a separate blocked_terms.txt file was created in the project root. The view reads this file on every request, meaning the list can be updated without touching the code or restarting the server. 

def load_blocked_terms():
    with open('blocked_terms.txt', 'r') as f:
        return [line.strip().lower() for line in f if line.strip()]

04 Write the Sentiment Analysis Logic

Articles from NewsAPI are formatted into a numbered list and sent to Claude with a carefully structured prompt. The prompt instructs Claude to label each article, ignore irrelevant ones, and return scores in a strict format that can be reliably parsed: 

SCORES:
positive: [number]
negative: [number]
neutral: [number]

SUMMARY:
Summary text here

05 Build the Charts with Chart.js

Django passes the sentiment scores to the HTML template as template variables. JavaScript reads these values and uses Chart.js to render a doughnut chart showing percentage breakdown and a bar chart showing raw article counts. Both charts are responsive and styled to match the site's dark theme.

06 Add the 3 Free Uses System

The same session based guest limit used on the summariser was applied here. Guest users get 3 free analyses before being redirected to the register page. Registered users have unlimited access and their usage is tracked in the database and displayed on their profile page.

5

Key Concepts Learned

Third Party API Integration

NewsAPI is the first data retrieval API used in this project — different from the Claude API which processes data. Combining a data source API with an AI processing API is a pattern used across many real world applications including financial dashboards, social media monitoring tools, and market research platforms.

Sentiment Analysis

Sentiment analysis is one of the most common real world applications of natural language processing. It is used in customer feedback systems, brand monitoring, financial trading signals, and social media analysis. Using Claude to perform sentiment analysis rather than a traditional NLP library gives more contextual and nuanced results.

Data Visualisation with Chart.js

Chart.js renders charts directly in the browser using HTML canvas elements. Django passes the data as template variables which JavaScript reads and uses to configure the charts. This pattern — server generates data, client renders visualisation — is the standard approach for web based dashboards.

Content Moderation via External File

Storing the blocked terms list in a separate text file rather than hardcoding it in the Python code follows the principle of separating configuration from logic. This makes the moderation list easier to update, review, and manage without risking changes to the application code.

Loose Coupling

Each function in views.py does exactly one job. The news retrieval, sentiment analysis, and response parsing are completely separate from each other. This means the news source could be swapped from NewsAPI to Bing News Search by changing only the get_news_articles function — nothing else would need to change.

6

Known Limitations

Keyword Matching Accuracy

NewsAPI uses keyword matching rather than semantic understanding. A search for "weather" may return articles where the word appears metaphorically — for example "weathering the economic storm". Claude's prompt instructs it to identify and ignore irrelevant articles in the summary, but they may still appear in the source articles list. Exact phrase matching using quoted keywords was implemented to reduce but not eliminate this issue.

NewsAPI Free Tier Restrictions

The free tier of NewsAPI is limited to 100 requests per day and only returns articles from the last month. It also does not provide access to full article content — only titles and descriptions. This means sentiment analysis is based on headlines and short summaries rather than complete articles, which may not fully represent the article's overall tone.

Small Sample Size

Results are based on a maximum of 10 articles. For topics with limited news coverage this means the sentiment scores may not be statistically representative. A production version would analyse significantly more articles and weight results by source credibility and recency.

Better News APIs Available

NewsAPI was chosen for its simplicity and free tier. More accurate alternatives include Bing News Search API (1,000 free requests per month, better relevance ranking), The Guardian API (free, editorially tagged articles, no irrelevant results), and the New York Times API (free, high quality journalism). A future version of this tool would use one of these alternatives.

7

Problems & Solutions

Problem 1 — Source articles showing raw HTML instead of rendered links

Problem

The source articles section was displaying raw HTML attribute text instead of rendered clickable links. The href, class, and other attributes were visible as plain text on the page.

Cause

VS Code's Prettier formatter was splitting HTML anchor tags across multiple lines. Django's template engine misinterpreted the split attributes and escaped them as plain text rather than rendering them as HTML.

Solution

Created a .prettierignore file in the project root containing **/*.html to prevent Prettier from formatting Django HTML templates. All anchor tags were moved onto single lines. This is a permanent fix — Prettier now leaves all HTML files untouched.

Problem 2 — Template tag error from mismatched if and endif

Error 

Invalid block tag on line 350: 'endif', expected 'empty' or 'endfor'

Cause

Prettier had placed the {% if %} and {% endif %} tags on the same line as surrounding content. Django's template parser could not correctly identify the block structure when tags were not on their own separate lines.

Solution

Ensured every Django template tag was on its own separate line. Django template tags must never be split across lines or merged onto the same line as other content. The .prettierignore fix prevents this from happening again automatically.

Problem 3 — Irrelevant articles appearing in search results

Problem

Searching for "weather" returned articles about sports, politics, and technology where the word "weather" appeared metaphorically in the text rather than as the main topic.

Cause

NewsAPI uses simple keyword matching across article titles, descriptions, and content. It has no understanding of context or relevance — it returns any article containing the search term regardless of whether it is the main subject.

Solution

Two measures were implemented. First the Claude prompt was updated to instruct Claude to identify and ignore irrelevant articles in its summary and scores. Second exact phrase matching was enabled in the NewsAPI query by wrapping the keyword in quotes. This reduced but did not fully eliminate irrelevant results — a known limitation of keyword based news APIs documented in the limitations section above.

8

Future Improvements

Switch to Bing News Search API

Bing News Search provides better relevance ranking and more accurate keyword matching than NewsAPI. Because the news retrieval logic is isolated in a single function, switching APIs would require changing only that one function with no impact on the rest of the codebase.

Search History

Store past searches and their results in the database so registered users can revisit previous analyses without making a new API call. This would also enable trend tracking — showing how sentiment around a topic changes over time.

Sentiment Trend Over Time

Add a line chart showing how sentiment around a keyword has changed day by day over the past week or month. This would require storing historical results and is a significant step towards a proper market intelligence tool.

Analyse More Articles

Currently limited to 10 articles due to API costs and token limits. A smarter approach would retrieve more articles, summarise them in batches, and combine the results for a more statistically representative sentiment score.

</>
